did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) combined with Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) and data augmentation to classify five types of skin cancer (dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, and actinic keratoses) using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model combined with SMOTE for classifying skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology, results (95% test accuracy), and comparative analysis with other models, which characterizes it as a research study rather than a review. Although it contains a literature survey section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical results.

title: Leveraging Convolutional Neural Networks for the Classification of Five Imbalanced Skin Cancers
journal: 2025 6th International Conference on Recent Advances in Information Technology (RAIT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of classifying five imbalanced types of skin cancer—dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, and actinic keratoses—using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers developed a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model enhanced with data augmentation and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) to mitigate class imbalance issues. The model was evaluated on test data, achieving an overall accuracy of 95%, with training accuracy reaching 99%. Performance metrics included precision ranging from 0.847 to 0.990 and F1-scores between 0.886 and 0.977, demonstrating robustness across the different classes. Comparative analysis indicated that the proposed CNN model with SMOTE outperformed other deep learning models in terms of accuracy, loss, and consistency. The results confirm the effectiveness of combining CNNs with SMOTE for reliable skin cancer classification and early detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the five classes studied in the experiment using the HAM10000 dataset. It had the highest number of images (6705) before augmentation.
One of the five classes studied. Described as a precancerous lesion that may develop into squamous cell carcinoma. Had 327 images before augmentation.
One of the five classes studied. Had 514 images before augmentation.
One of the five classes studied. Described as benign anomalies containing blood vessels. Had 142 images before augmentation.
One of the five classes studied. Described as benign and compound nodules often confused for malignancies. Had 115 images before augmentation.

Mentioned in the introduction as a major cause of skin cancer fatalities and in the literature survey as a class in other studies (e.g., Sulthana and Chamola [1]), but explicitly excluded from the five classes examined in this specific study to address imbalance issues.

all_task_types.target: Five imbalanced skin cancer classes (dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, and actinic keratoses)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on the classification of five distinct diagnostic categories of skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete class labels (df, vasc, nv, bcc, akiec) rather than continuous values. Performance was evaluated using accuracy (95% test accuracy), precision, recall, and F1-scores, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset accessed via Kaggle and Google Drive, containing annotated and high-quality images of skin lesions. The study utilized a subset of this dataset focusing on five specific classes to address class imbalance issues.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model after data augmentation and SMOTE application.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the model's performance (95% test accuracy).
use.other: The dataset originally contains 7 classes, but this study focused on 5 classes to address imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores, and loss curves, with comparative analysis based on metric values rather than statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: Custom CNN with SMOTE and Data Augmentation
HAM10000.split: 80/20 Train/Validation split (implied test set used for final metrics)

akiec.notes: Lowest precision; confused with bcc and nv

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model showed superiority in accuracy, loss, and consistency among the five classes compared to other deep learning models. The akiec class had the lowest precision (0.847) but was still considered fairly classifiable. The model achieved equal accuracy to Hybrid CNN-DenseNet but with lower complexity.

architecture_details.convolutional_layers: 8 Conv2D layers with (3,3) kernel size and ReLU activation
architecture_details.normalization: Batch Normalization layers after each Conv2D layer
architecture_details.flatten_layer: Flatten layer converting 2D feature maps to 1D vectors

classification_or_output_head.purpose: Multi-class classification for 5 skin cancer classes

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization: Standardizing pixel values to a consistent range [0, 1].
Resizing: Resizing original images (600x450 pixels) to 64x64 pixels for consistent dimensions.
Filtering: Using filtering to remove noisy or irrelevant images to ensure quality.
Label Encoding: Converting categorical class labels (akiec, bcc, df, nv, vasc) into numerical formats (0, 1, 2, 3, 4).
Data Augmentation: Applying rotation, translation, shearing, zooming, flipping, and brightness adjustment to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting.
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique): Generating synthetic samples for minority classes to address class imbalance after augmentation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied rotation, translation, shearing, zooming, flipping, and brightness adjustment to increase dataset diversity and reduce overfitting. Specific probabilities or ranges were not reported.

Used to address class imbalance by generating synthetic samples for minority classes. The method selects samples from the minority class, finds k-nearest neighbors, and generates synthetic images along lines connecting the sample and its neighbors. Applied after geometric augmentation.

conclusions: The proposed custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model combined with data augmentation and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) effectively addresses class imbalance issues in skin cancer classification.

The model achieved a training accuracy of 99% and a test accuracy of 95% on the HAM10000 dataset for five imbalanced skin cancer classes (dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, and actinic keratoses).

The proposed CNN model demonstrated superiority in accuracy, loss, and consistency compared to other deep learning models such as Residual Network-50 (76.07%), S-MobileNet (93.0%), and standard CNNs (91.0%-93.3%).
The proposed model achieved equal accuracy (95.8%) to the Hybrid CNN-DenseNet model (95.7%) but with significantly lower complexity and computational resource demands.
The model showed robust performance across classes, with precision ranging from 0.847 to 0.990 and F1-scores between 0.886 and 0.977.

The actinic keratoses (akiec) class had the lowest precision (0.847) and was frequently confused with basal cell carcinoma (bcc) and melanocytic nevi (nv), indicating a specific area for model improvement.
The custom CNN architecture reduced model complexity compared to hybrid and pre-trained models while preventing overfitting through the use of SMOTE and augmentation techniques.

The study acknowledges the limitation of low precision for the akiec class and suggests future work to classify all seven classes using a fusion of handcrafted features with CNN models and to develop real-time applications.
important_remarks: The study explicitly excludes melanoma, the most lethal skin cancer, focusing only on five other classes (dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, and actinic keratoses), limiting clinical relevance for early detection of fatal cases.

The dataset is highly imbalanced (e.g., 6,705 melanocytic nevi vs. 115 dermatofibroma images), and while SMOTE was used, the minority classes (especially dermatofibroma and vascular lesions) remain at risk of overfitting or synthetic data artifacts.

The actinic keratoses (akiec) class had the lowest precision (0.847) and was frequently confused with basal cell carcinoma and melanocytic nevi, indicating significant diagnostic ambiguity between these precancerous and cancerous lesions.
Images were resized from 600x450 to 64x64 pixels, which may result in loss of fine dermoscopic features critical for accurate classification of subtle skin lesions.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for the comparison between the proposed model and baseline models, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy and loss.

The model was trained and evaluated on a single public dataset (HAM10000) without external validation on independent clinical datasets, raising concerns about generalizability to diverse skin types and imaging devices.

The study acknowledges that the custom CNN architecture, while effective, is a simplified approach compared to hybrid or pre-trained models, potentially limiting its performance ceiling on complex diagnostic tasks.
The paper does not address potential biases in the HAM10000 dataset regarding skin tone diversity, which is a known limitation in dermatological AI research.
